perturbation.original_text: For purposes of this Agreement, "Confidential Information" shall include all information or material that has or could have commercial value or other utility in the business in which Disclosing Party is engaged.
perturbation.changed_text: For purposes of this Agreement, "Confidential Information" shall include only information explicitly marked as confidential, regardless of its potential commercial value or utility in the business in which Disclosing Party is engaged.
perturbation.explanation: Redefining "Confidential Information" to require explicit marking contradicts established trade secret law, which protects information based on its inherent value and reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy, not solely on whether it's explicitly labeled. This shift increases the burden of proof and makes trade secret protection uncertain.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text contradicts the UTSA definition of 'trade secret', which doesn't necessitate explicit marking, focusing instead on commercial value and reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. By requiring explicit marking, the agreement excludes information that would otherwise qualify as a trade secret under the UTSA.

perturbation.justification: The modified text redefines "Confidential Information" to require explicit marking, which contradicts the Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA) by narrowing the scope of protection. The Law Explanation states the UTSA definition of trade secret doesn't need explicit marking, focusing on commercial value and reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. The change requires explicit marking, excluding information qualifying as a trade secret under the UTSA. Scraped Law Snippet 2 references the Uniform Law Commission which created the UTSA. Therefore, the modified contract language directly undermines trade secret protection by adding a requirement not present in the governing law.

perturbation.original_text: Receiving Party shall hold and maintain the Confidential Information in strictest confidence for the sole and exclusive benefit of the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: Receiving Party shall endeavor to hold and maintain the Confidential Information in reasonable confidence for the general benefit of both the Disclosing Party and Receiving Party.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'strictest confidence' to 'reasonable confidence' and introducing a 'general benefit' standard weakens the obligation to protect confidential information, contradicting the expected level of care in trade secret protection and creating ambiguity about the Receiving Party's duties.
perturbation.law_explanation: Restatement of Torts § 757 outlines liability for the disclosure or use of another's trade secret. The original text aligns with the high duty of care required to protect trade secrets. Modifying it to 'reasonable confidence' lowers the standard and introduces a 'general benefit', undermining the principle that trade secrets are exclusively for the benefit of the owner, thus contradicting established tort law regarding trade secret misappropriation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: g those for theft, damage to property, deposit, and production of property. All of these could, I suppose, be used to protect trade secrets, etc., but there is no evidence they were. It is bizarre to see any degree the Roman actio servi corrupti as the counterpart of modern law for the protection of trade secrets and other such commercial interests. [ 10 ] Early case law [ edit ] Modern trade secret law is primarily rooted in Anglo-American common law . [ 11 ] (p6) The earliest recorded court case was the 1817 English case Newbery v. James, which involved a secret formula for gout treatment. [ 12 ] [ 11 ] (p5) [ 13 ] In the United States, this concept was first recognized in the 1837 case Vickery v. Welch , involving the sale of a chocolate factory and the seller’s agreement to keep the secret recipe confidential. [ 14 ] [ 15 ] Newbery and Vickery only awarded compensation for losses ( damages ) and did not issue orders to prevent the misuse of secrets ( injunctive relief ). [ 11 ] (p5) The first English case involving injunctive relief was Yovatt v. Winyard in 1820, where the court issued an injunction to prevent a former employee from using or disclosing recipes he had secretly copied from his employer's veterinary medicine practice. [ 16 ] [ 17 ] In the United States, the 1868 Massachusetts Supreme Court decision in Peabody v. Norfolk is one of the most well-known and well-reasoned early trade secret case, establishing foundational legal principles that continue to be central to common law. [ 18 ] [ 19 ] In this case, the court ruled that Peabody’s confidential manufacturing process was a protectable trade secret and issued an injunction preventing former employees from using or disclosing it after they shared it with a competitor. [ 18 ] Uniform lawmaking and legislation [ edit ] In 1939, the Restatement of Torts, published by the American Law Institute , offered, among other things, one of the earliest formal definitions of a trade secret. According to Section 757, Comment b, a trade secret may consist of "any formula, pattern, device, or compilation of information which is used in one's business, and which gives the business an opportunity to obtain an advantage over competitors who do not know or use it." [ 20 ] (p278) This definition became widely used by courts across the United States. [ 20 ] (p278) As the first attempt to outline the accepted principles of trade secret law, the Restatement served as the primary authority adopted in virtually every reported case. [ 20 ] (p282) Trade secret law saw further development in 1979 when the Uniform Law Commission (ULC) introduced a model law known as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA), which was later amended in 1985. The UTSA defines the types of information eligible for trade secret protection, establishes a private cause of action for misappropriation, and outlines remedies such as injunctions, damages, and, in certain cases, attorneys' fees. [ 21 ] It has since been adopted by 48 states, along with the District of Columbia, Puerto Rico, and the U.S. Virgin Islands, with New York and North Carolina as the exceptions. [ 22 ] [ 23 ] The UTSA influenced the Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA) of 2016, which created a federal civil cause of action for trade secret misappropriation, allowing plaintiffs to file cases directly in federal courts if "the trade secret is related to a product or service used in ... interstate or foreign commerce." [ 23 ] International standards [ edit ] Trade secret law is governed by national legal systems. [ 24 ] However, international standards for protecting secrets (called “undisclosed information”) were established as part of the TRIPS Agreement in 1995. [ 24 ] Article 39 of TRIPS obligates member countries to protect “undisclosed information” from unauthorized use conducted “in a manner contrary to honest commercial practices,” including actions such as breach of contract, breach of confidence, and unfair competition. perturbation.justification: The original text requires 'strictest confidence' for the 'sole and exclusive benefit' of the Disclosing Party. The changed text weakens this to 'reasonable confidence' for the 'general benefit' of both parties. The Law Explanation indicates that this contradicts trade secret law, where the duty of care is high and trade secrets are exclusively for the owner's benefit. Scraped Law Snippet 2 mentions Restatement of Torts § 757, which defines a trade secret as giving a business an advantage over competitors. By weakening the obligation and introducing a 'general benefit', the changed text undermines the exclusive nature of trade secret protection, creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Receiving Party shall return to Disclosing Party any and all records, notes, and other written, printed, or tangible materials in its possession pertaining to Confidential Information immediately if Disclosing Party requests it in writing.
perturbation.changed_text: Receiving Party shall return to Disclosing Party such records, notes, and other written, printed, or tangible materials in its possession pertaining to Confidential Information that the Disclosing Party reasonably requests within a commercially reasonable timeframe.
perturbation.explanation: Adding 'reasonably requests' and 'commercially reasonable timeframe' introduces subjectivity and delays in the return of confidential information, which is inconsistent with the immediate return requirement necessary to protect trade secrets. This hinders the Disclosing Party's ability to control and safeguard its sensitive information.
perturbation.law_explanation: The DTSA aims to protect trade secrets from misappropriation. The original text ensures swift return of confidential information, crucial for maintaining its secrecy. The modified text allows for delays and introduces a reasonableness standard, weakening the protection against misappropriation and potentially allowing for continued use or disclosure during the 'commercially reasonable timeframe', thus contradicting the DTSA's intent.

Civil proceedings (a) The Attorney General may, in a civil action, obtain appropriate injunctive relief against any violation of this chapter. (b) Private Civil Actions.- (1) In general .-An owner of a trade secret that is misappropriated may bring a civil action under this subsection if the trade secret is related to a product or service used in, or intended for use in, interstate or foreign commerce. (2) Civil seizure.- (A) In
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates an immediate return of confidential information. The changed text introduces a 'commercially reasonable timeframe' and 'reasonably requests,' which allows for delays. This directly contradicts the intent of swiftly protecting trade secrets, as highlighted in the Law Explanation. While the provided law snippet primarily addresses civil proceedings related to trade secret misappropriation (18 USC 1836), the essence of protecting trade secrets hinges on preventing unauthorized use and disclosure. Delaying the return of confidential information, even for a 'commercially reasonable timeframe', increases the risk of misappropriation, effectively weakening the protection that the original text provided, and contradicting the purpose of trade secret laws.

perturbation.original_text: Time Periods. The nondisclosure provisions of this Agreement shall survive the termination of this Agreement and Receiving Party's duty to hold Confidential Information in confidence shall remain in effect until the Confidential Information no longer qualifies as a trade secret or until Disclosing Party sends Receiving Party written notice releasing Receiving Party from this Agreement, whichever occurs first.
perturbation.changed_text: Time Periods. The nondisclosure provisions of this Agreement shall survive the termination of this Agreement and Receiving Party's duty to hold Confidential Information in confidence shall remain in effect for a period of one year following the termination of this Agreement, regardless of whether the Confidential Information qualifies as a trade secret.
perturbation.explanation: Limiting the non-disclosure period to one year contradicts trade secret law, which protects trade secrets for as long as they remain secret and provide a competitive advantage. This change eliminates indefinite protection, regardless of ongoing secrecy, thus reducing the scope and duration of trade secret protection.
perturbation.law_explanation: Restatement (Third) of Unfair Competition § 39 indicates the scope and duration of trade secret protection is based on the continued secrecy and commercial value of the information. By arbitrarily limiting the non-disclosure period to one year, the modified text disregards the ongoing status of the information as a trade secret, contradicting the Restatement's guidance on the appropriate duration of protection.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract contradicts trade secret law by limiting the non-disclosure period to one year, regardless of whether the confidential information still qualifies as a trade secret. The original text protected the information as long as it qualified as a trade secret or until the disclosing party released the receiving party from the agreement. The modified text eliminates this indefinite protection, directly contradicting the principle that trade secrets are protected as long as they remain secret and provide a competitive advantage. The Law Explanation supports this, stating that Restatement (Third) of Unfair Competition § 39 indicates the scope and duration of trade secret protection is based on the continued secrecy and commercial value of the information. While the scraped snippets are unhelpful, the Perturbation and Law Explanations, as well as the comparison of the original and changed text, strongly indicate a contradiction.
